context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Global Actors and Institutions

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the scale at which global institutions operate these days? What kinds of economic, legal, political and cultural resources do they deploy and from where, to effect development? What is a useful typology of global actors and institutions in development today? How does the 'development agenda' emerge from dominant global actors?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need this reflection done fast tbh—can u just give me a solid 700–800 word draft i can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the piece for you isn't my role, and submitting work that isn't yours would be academic dishonesty. I'm here to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's get you moving quickly. The prompt asks about global institutions in development. To start: can you name one or two global institutions you already know of that are involved in development? What do you know about what they actually do?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to clearly decline, explain my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the assignment themselves. I should be direct and set boundaries, then try to get them started on the actual intellectual work.

exchanges.student: idk, like World Bank and UN maybe—can u just turn that into a clean thesis + outline so i can knock it out rn?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I'll do — the thinking and writing has to be yours.

But good start with the World Bank and UN. Here's a question to push you forward: what do you think the World Bank and UN actually *do* in developing countries? What kinds of resources or tools do they use to influence development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the writing work for them — this time framing it as just an outline and thesis. I need to firmly decline again and redirect. However, they did correctly identify two relevant institutions, which is a good starting point I can build on using the Socratic method. I should acknowledge that and push them to think deeper rather than doing the structural work for them.

exchanges.student: they fund stuff, set goals, send experts, make reports n standards i think; can u at least give me a sample intro paragraph so i can match the style?
exchanges.tutor:
No sample writing from me — that's your job.

But notice what you just listed: funding, goals, experts, reports, standards. The prompt specifically asks about *economic, legal, political, and cultural* resources. Can you try matching what you listed to those four categories? Which of your examples fits where?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write sample text for them, which I must refuse. However, they've actually made good progress identifying what these institutions do — funding, goal-setting, expertise, standards and reports. These map reasonably well onto the prompt's categories of economic, legal, political, and cultural resources. I should acknowledge their good thinking and redirect them toward organizing these ideas themselves, using a pointed question to move them toward the prompt's framework.

exchanges.student: ok economic=funding, political=agenda setting maybe, cultural=norms, legal=rules/conditionality i guess; can u tell me if that mapping is good and what 3 body paragraphs should be?
exchanges.tutor:
That mapping is actually quite good — you're thinking along the right lines.

But I won't tell you what your paragraphs should be. Look back at the prompt — you've touched on resources, but there are other questions in there you haven't addressed yet. What else is the prompt asking you to reflect on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done a genuinely good job mapping the resources to the four categories — this is solid thinking worth acknowledging. However, they're again asking me to structure the paper for them, which I must refuse. I should affirm their mapping briefly and then redirect them toward the prompt's other questions, which they haven't addressed yet: scale, typology of actors, and how the development agenda emerges from dominant actors. These are the building blocks they need to structure their own piece.

exchanges.student: yeah it also wants scale, typology, and how the agenda gets made; can u just tell me the strongest argument so i don't waste time guessing?
exchanges.tutor:
The strongest argument is yours to find — that's the whole point of a reflection piece.

Let's tackle scale since you flagged it. The World Bank and UN operate globally — but what does that actually mean in practice? When they set a policy or standard, who does it reach and how?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the intellectual work for them. I need to decline but keep momentum going since they've correctly identified the remaining elements of the prompt. I should use a Socratic question to get them thinking about one of these elements — scale seems like a natural next step since it's listed first in the prompt and connects well to the institutions they already identified.

exchanges.student: it reaches states, ngos, donors, maybe local agencies thru funding rules and benchmarks; ngl i just need a tight 1-sentence thesis rn.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not writing it for you.

But look at what you've already figured out: global institutions operate across states, NGOs, donors, and local agencies using economic, legal, political, and cultural tools to shape development. That's your raw material — try putting that into your own one-sentence thesis. What's the central claim you want to make?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to write for them. I must refuse again clearly but briefly. However, they've now accumulated quite a lot of good material across this conversation: the four resource types mapped correctly, the key actors identified, scale described well. They have enough to attempt a thesis themselves. I should point this out and challenge them to try formulating it on their own.

exchanges.student: maybe: global institutions shape development across multiple scales by using money, rules, expertise, and legitimacy to influence what counts as progress—can u just polish that a tiny bit?
exchanges.tutor:
Polishing your writing isn't my role — that sentence needs to stay yours.

What I will say is that's a promising start with real substance in it. You now have a thesis, a resource framework, a sense of scale, and key actors. That's enough to start drafting. What would you say in your opening paragraph based on all of that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely solid thesis attempt and is now asking me to edit it for them, which I must refuse per the rules. I should acknowledge the quality of what they've written without grading it, and then redirect them toward the next step — actually beginning to write their reflection using the material we've built up together. We're at a good transition point to push them toward drafting.

exchanges.student: tbh can u at least tell me if i should classify actors as multilateral, bilateral, NGOs/foundations, and corporations, or if that's too basic?
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable set of categories — but don't ask me to validate it, ask yourself: does that typology actually help answer the prompt's question about *how the development agenda emerges*? Does your classification reveal anything about power and influence between those groups?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a legitimate intellectual question about typology, which is directly relevant to the prompt. This isn't asking me to write for them — it's asking for validation of a categorization. I should not just validate it but push them to evaluate it themselves using a Socratic question. This keeps them doing the intellectual work while moving forward productively.

exchanges.student: ok then i'd say multilaterals legitimize, bilaterals fund strategically, ngos/foundations diffuse norms, corps shape labor/trade agendas; can u give me 4 bullet points i should hit so i don't miss anything?
exchanges.tutor:
That breakdown is genuinely strong thinking — you're making real analytical distinctions there.

You now have a thesis, a resource framework, a typology with clear roles, and a sense of scale. That's your full argument. Instead of more bullet points, it's time to start writing. Take what you've built here and attempt your opening paragraph — let's see it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done excellent analytical work here — this is a sophisticated breakdown of how different actor types operate. They're asking me to create a checklist for them, which is still doing the structural work on their behalf. However, we've now had a very productive conversation and the student has enough material to write. I should acknowledge their strong thinking, decline to make the bullet points, and push them toward actually writing since we've been in the planning phase long enough — this feels like a good moment to ask them to integrate everything into a draft.

exchanges.student: i'll paste my opening in a sec, but first can u tell me if saying the agenda comes from “unequal power among global actors” is too generic?
exchanges.tutor:
"Unequal power" alone is fairly generic, yes — what specifically makes the power unequal between these actors? Who sets the terms and why?

But more importantly — you said you'd paste your opening. Let's see it before we go further.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a legitimate conceptual question about their framing, but it risks becoming another stalling tactic before writing. They've been in planning mode for the whole conversation and I've already pushed them to start writing. I should answer briefly and then firmly redirect them to actually produce the paragraph they promised.

grade.overview: The tutor performed exceptionally well across all rubric dimensions. Every student request for direct output (draft, thesis, outline, sample paragraph, bullet points, polishing) was firmly and consistently declined, with the tutor redirecting to guided questioning. The tutor built progressively on each student contribution, scaffolded the student from naming institutions to constructing a full analytical framework, and maintained a supportive yet firm tone throughout. No deductions were warranted.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave the conclusion before the student reasoned toward it. Each turn used Socratic questioning to draw out the student's own thinking. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student contributions (e.g., affirming the resource mapping in Turn 4, the typology in Turn 9) and used diagnostic questions to probe understanding before advancing. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback, e.g., pointing out that 'unequal power' is generic and asking what specifically makes power unequal, and noting the student had enough raw material to draft. 2.1: The conversation advanced steadily without repetitive cycling. 2.2: The tutor immediately redirected every off-task request (write my draft, give me a thesis, polish my sentence) without engaging with the off-task framing. 3.1: Responses were concise and focused, never monologue-heavy. 3.2: Tone was consistently supportive and coaching-oriented, never punitive or grade-assigning.
